Read it: "the early 21st century has been full of desperate attempts at refusing to have a 21st century."
It's a good line. But my honest read is that the pattern holds so completely across history that it actually inverts what the post implies. Refusing the new century isn't a feature of this century. It's the standard way societies metabolize epochal change. So the aphorism names a mood beautifully and periodizes poorly.
The evidence, since you asked specifically about the 1800s/1900s:
1800s. The Luddites (1811-16) literally smashed the new century's machines. The Congress of Vienna in 1815 was a fully explicit political project to refuse the French Revolution and bolt the pre-revolutionary order back together; it bought a few decades and then collapsed. Romanticism was in large part a refusal of industrial rationalization. The slaveholding South built an entire civilization-ideology around refusing the moral trajectory of its own era.
1900s. The Scopes trial (1925) was a courtroom staged to refuse Darwinian modernity. Prohibition was a refusal of modern pluralist urban culture. The 1924 Immigration Act was a refusal of the demographic century. And fascism was what Jeffrey Herf called "reactionary modernism": using the new century's technology to refuse its values. That last one matters most.

And the structure was named long ago: Polanyi's "double movement" (market society automatically provokes a protective counter-movement), Hirschman's Rhetoric of Reaction, Corey Robin's Reactionary Mind. Reaction is constitutive of modernity, not incidental to it. Go back further and the Counter-Reformation is the same shape.
So yes, the pattern holds, robustly, every time. But here's where it gets interesting, two turns:
What might actually be distinctive now. The thing being refused is less refusable than it has ever been. Past reactions could sometimes win, or at least freeze change for generations. The Restoration bought decades. Jim Crow bought a century. But you cannot Congress-of-Vienna the carbon back out of the atmosphere, and you cannot un-train the models. The 21st century's defining conditions are planetary and partly physically irreversible in a way that contingent social arrangements never were. That's what earns the word "desperate." The desperation scales with the irreversibility of what's being refused. A reaction that can still plausibly win is angry. A reaction against thermodynamics is desperate.

The framing is the thing to question. "Having versus refusing the century" is a false binary, and it's the kind I can't help poking. The Counter-Reformation didn't fail to have the early modern period; it helped build it. The reactionaries aren't standing outside the 21st century declining delivery. Their refusal is one of the century's defining products. The century isn't being refused. It's being constituted partly out of the refusals. So the truest version of the post isn't "people keep refusing the 21st century." It's: the 21st century's actual content includes its own desperate disavowal. The refusal is a mode of having it.
So: sharp post, real mood, but it reads better as a description of a perennial law than a diagnosis of now. The genuinely new part isn't the refusing. It's that this time the thing being refused doesn't negotiate.

Following a tip from ChatGPT, the FBI alerted Brazilian police about a father's plan to kill his son to avoid paying child support in Espírito Santo.
The suspect attempted to hire a hitman for R$ 50,000 and also revealed a desire to attack police officers and carry out acts of violence in public places. He was arrested in São Gabriel da Palha, in the north of Espírito Santo, on the 19th of last month

A dark-themed tracking dashboard titled "Strait of Hormuz Reopen Date". The header outlines a forecasting question regarding when the strait will reopen to normal traffic (defined as ≥80% of 2024-2025 baseline volume sustained for ≥30 days). Metadata below lists Status: ACTIVE, Health: DEGRADED, Updated: 2026-06-27, and an expected value of "E[days from 2026-05-03] 433.48". Under the "OVERVIEW" tab, a section titled "ACTIVE PRIORS" describes the active posterior updating only via approved typed commits. It displays probability bars for four hypotheses:
​H1 (Reopens by 2026-09-30): Shows a small cyan progress bar.
​H2 (Reopens 2026-10-01 to 2027-03-31): Shows a small magenta progress bar.
​H3 (Reopens 2027-04-01 to 2027-12-31): Shows a larger amber progress bar.
​H4 (Not reopened by 2027-12-31): Shows an empty tracking bar.

A dark-themed data dashboard interface titled "SHADOW VS ACTIVE". The top section contains an explanatory paragraph defining "Shadow" as an independent probability estimate based on elapsed time (how long things take) rather than news evidence, specifically noting a strait closure of 65 days. Below the text, a legend shows "active (committed)" in cyan and "shadow (dynamics-derived)" in magenta. Four hypotheses (H1 through H4) are listed with horizontal comparison bars and percentage-point deltas:
​H1 (Reopens by 2026-09-30): Small active bar vs small shadow bar (+0.2pp in amber).
​H2 (Reopens 2026-10-01 to 2027-03-31): Small active bar vs small shadow bar (+6.4pp in amber).
​H3 (Reopens 2027-04-01 to 2027-12-31): Large active bar vs small shadow bar (-55.4pp in cyan).
​H4 (Not reopened by 2027-12-31): Empty active bar vs moderate shadow bar (+48.7pp in amber).
A footnote at the bottom notes that SHADOW is a guide, not authoritative, and defines positive delta (amber) as the shadow model thinking the hypothesis is more likely than committed, and negative delta (cyan) as less likely.

The weather was unusually hot that summer and Wittgenstein's room on the second floor was often very uncomfortable. He once pointed out to me that the wire mesh of the window screens restricted to some extent the movement of air through his win-dows, and wondered why they could not be removed. I replied that if this were done great numbers of insects would enter and prove even more disagreeable than the heat. Wittgenstein doubted that this was so. He remarked that in England and on the Continent windows were commonly left unscreened. I answered that there were more insects in America. Wittgenstein didn't believe this, and when he went out for a walk later that day he made a point of looking at a number of houses in order to see whether their windows were screened. He found that all of them were but, oddly enough, instead of inferring that there must be a good reason for it, he concluded with some irritation that Americans were the victims of widespread and unthinking prejudice as to the necessity of window screens!

Three comic panels. Top panel: Two duck characters in ancient Greek attire converse. Left, a blond duck asks a white-haired duck in a red cape, "Manage the kingdom! Does that appeal to you, Uncle Aeson?" Middle panel: The white-haired duck (Scrooge McDuck) joyfully hugs the Golden Fleece, with hearts floating around him. His speech bubble reads: "And my first act is to decree that this fleece is henceforth the official cape of the King's Treasurer!" Bottom panel (partially visible): Another duck's speech bubble declares, "Thanks to his luck, our city will become the richest in all of Greece!"
